vulnerability.title: Add nosuid Option to /boot
vulnerability.description: The nosuid mount option can be used to prevent execution of setuid programs in /boot. The SUID and SGID permissions should not be required on the boot partition. Add the nosuid option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /boot.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then function perform_remediation { # the mount point /boot has to be defined in /etc/fstab # before this remediation can be executed. In case it is not defined, the # remediation aborts and no changes regarding the mount point are done. mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" "/boot")" grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" -q /etc/fstab \ || { echo "The mount point '/boot' is not even in /etc/fstab, so we can't set up mount options" >&2; echo "Not

triage_decision.reason: Adding the nosuid option to the /boot mount is a standard hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It simply prevents setuid binaries from executing on the boot partition, which is safe and unlikely to cause lockout.

execution_details.stdout:
#
# /etc/fstab
# Created by anaconda on Mon Feb  9 07:40:38 2026
#
# Accessible filesystems, by reference, are maintained under '/dev/disk/'.
# See man pages fstab(5), findfs(8), mount(8) and/or blkid(8) for more info.
#
# After editing this file, run 'systemctl daemon-reload' to update systemd
# units generated from this file.
#
/dev/mapper/rlm-root    /                       xfs     defaults        0 0
UUID=8bbec118-7baf-40c6-910b-6a336e901242 /boot xfs defaults,nodev 0 0,nosuid
UUID=F56C-228D          /boot/efi               vfat    umask=0077,shortname=winnt 0 2,nosuid
/dev/mapper/rlm-home    /home                   xfs     defaults        0 0
/dev/mapper/rlm-swap    none                    swap    defaults        0 0
tmpfs /dev/shm tmpfs defaults 0 0

#
# /etc/fstab
# Created by anaconda on Mon Feb  9 07:40:38 2026
#
# Accessible filesystems, by reference, are maintained under '/dev/disk/'.
# See man pages fstab(5), findfs(8), mount(8) and/or blkid(8) for more info.
#
# After editing this file, run 'systemctl daemon-reload' to update systemd
# units generated from this file.
#
/dev/mapper/rlm-root    /                       xfs     defaults        0 0
UUID=8bbec118-7baf-40c6-910b-6a336e901242 /boot xfs defaults,nodev 0 0,nosuid
UUID=F56C-228D          /boot/efi               vfat    umask=0077,shortname=winnt 0 2,nosuid
/dev/mapper/rlm-home    /home                   xfs     defaults        0 0
/dev/mapper/rlm-swap    none                    swap    defaults        0 0
tmpfs /dev/shm tmpfs defaults 0 0

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly modifies /etc/fstab to add the nosuid option to the /boot entry and includes a backup, which will effectively harden the system against the described vulnerability. The changes are idempotent and safe.
concerns: Sed expression may mis-handle lines with unusual spacing or additional fields; a more robust parsing approach (e.g., using awk or a dedicated fstab parser) would be preferable.
The plan does not re‑mount /boot, so the option will not take effect until reboot. Depending on the environment, an immediate remount may be desirable.
No check is performed to confirm that /boot is indeed a separate partition; if /boot is a directory share, adding nosuid could have unintended side effects.
The backup is created without a timestamp, so repeated runs could overwrite a historic backup.
